TRIGGERS: Physical pressure on tumor, certain foods (tyramine), anesthesia, certain medications. DIAGNOSIS: 24-hour urine collection for metanephrines, normetanephrines, VMA (vanillylmandelic acid). Plasma free metanephrines (high sensitivity). Imaging: CT or MRI adrenals, MIBG scan (radiotracer taken up by chromaffin cells). TREATMENT: Surgical excision is curative. Preoperative preparation is CRITICAL: (1) Alpha-blockade FIRST (phenoxybenzamine 10-14 days before surgery) — controls BP, allows vascular volume expansion. (2) Beta-blockade SECOND (propranolol) — controls tachycardia. NEVER reverse this order! (3) High-salt diet, volume expansion. Intraoperative: expect hypertensive crisis during tumor manipulation, hypotension after tumor removal (suddenly no catecholamines). Let yourself be drawn into the '10% tumor': 10% bilateral, 10% extra-adrenal, 10% malignant, 10% familial (MEN 2A/2B, von Hippel-Lindau, neurofibromatosis, hereditary paraganglioma syndromes).
mechanism.metaphor: A pheochromocytoma is like a rogue sympathetic fire alarm that goes off randomly, flooding your body with emergency hormones when there's no emergency. Let me show you: every time the alarm sounds, your body mobilizes for a threat that doesn't exist — blood pressure skyrockets, heart pounds, you break into a sweat. the only way to stop the false alarms is to surgically remove the broken alarm system. But first, you must prepare the body to survive one last massive surge when you touch the tumor during surgery. Isn't that a dangerous dance?

questions.q: An asthmatic patient is started on propranolol (non-selective β-blocker) for hypertension. Two days later, he develops severe bronchospasm requiring hospitalization. What receptor blockade caused this crisis?
questions.explain: Come with me into this preventable tragedy. Propranolol blocks BOTH β1 and β2 receptors. Blocking β2 on bronchial smooth muscle removes the sympathetic bronchodilation — the airways constrict. In an asthmatic whose airways are already hyperreactive, this can be catastrophic. Let me show you why β-selectivity matters.

prereqs.q: β2 receptors on bronchial smooth muscle mediate bronchodilation.
prereqs.explain: Sympathetic activation and circulating epinephrine relax bronchial smooth muscle via β2 receptors, opening the airways. this is why β2 agonists (albuterol) are rescue medications for asthma. The β2 receptor keeps airways open. Block it, and they close.

prereqs.q: β-blockers can have different selectivity for β1 versus β2 receptor subtypes.
prereqs.explain: Non-selective β-blockers (propranolol, nadolol) block BOTH β1 and β2. Cardioselective β-blockers (metoprolol, atenolol) preferentially block β1. this selectivity matters when patients have both cardiac disease AND pulmonary disease. Choose the wrong drug, and you harm the lungs trying to help the heart.

optionExplains.text: α1 blockade causes vasodilation and can lower blood pressure, but it doesn't affect bronchial tone directly. Propranolol doesn't block α1 receptors anyway — it's a β-blocker. Wrong receptor.

β1 blockade slows the heart and reduces cardiac output — useful for hypertension and heart disease. But β1 blockade doesn't cause bronchospasm. Different tissue distribution. The problem is in the LUNGS, not the heart.

Correct! Let me take you through this dangerous drug interaction. Propranolol is a NON-SELECTIVE β-blocker — it blocks β1 AND β2. Here's what happens: β2 receptors on bronchial smooth muscle normally mediate relaxation (bronchodilation). Blocking them removes this relaxation → bronchoconstriction. In a HEALTHY person, this might be tolerable. In an ASTHMATIC whose airways are hyperreactive and inflamed, blocking β2 receptors tips the balance toward dangerous airway constriction. the asthmatic relies on endogenous catecholamines AND β2 agonist medications (rescue inhalers) to keep airways open. Block the receptor, and even the rescue inhaler won't work as well. Come closer: this is why non-selective β-blockers are CONTRAINDICATED in asthmatics. If a β-blocker is absolutely necessary, use a cardioselective one (metoprolol, atenolol) that preferentially spares β2 — though even these should be used with extreme caution. Here's the lesson: know your patient's lungs before prescribing cardiac medications. Isn't this a beautiful example of why receptor selectivity matters?

Muscarinic receptor blockade (like atropine) would cause BRONCHODILATION, not bronchoconstriction — parasympathetic blockade opposes bronchoconstriction. Propranolol doesn't block muscarinic receptors anyway. Wrong mechanism entirely.

mechanism.title: β-Blocker-Induced Bronchospasm: Receptor Selectivity Matters
mechanism.content: Let yourself be immersed in this pharmacological disaster. NORMAL β2 FUNCTION: Sympathetic activation and circulating epinephrine → β2 receptors on bronchial smooth muscle → Gs → increased cAMP → relaxation → bronchodilation. This opposes parasympathetic bronchoconstriction (M3 receptors) and inflammatory mediators. ASTHMA PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: Airway hyperreactivity, inflammation, baseline increased smooth muscle tone. Patients depend on β2-mediated bronchodilation to maintain adequate airflow. Many use β2 agonist inhalers (albuterol) as rescue medication. β-BLOCKER EFFECT: NON-SELECTIVE (propranolol, nadolol, timolol) — block BOTH β1 (cardiac) and β2 (pulmonary, vascular). Blocking β2 → loss of bronchodilation → unopposed bronchoconstriction. In asthmatics, this can trigger severe bronchospasm, respiratory distress, even death. CARDIOSELECTIVE (metoprolol, atenolol, bisoprolol) — preferentially block β1, relatively spare β2. Still have SOME β2 blockade, especially at higher doses. Safer in patients with reactive airway disease but NOT risk-free. Come with me into the clinical implications: ABSOLUTE CONTRAINDICATIONS for non-selective β-blockers — asthma, severe COPD, acute bronchospasm. RELATIVE CONTRAINDICATIONS for cardioselective β-blockers — use lowest effective dose, monitor closely. ALTERNATIVES for hypertension in asthmatics — ACE inhibitors, ARBs, calcium channel blockers, diuretics. even TOPICAL β-blockers for glaucoma (timolol eye drops) can be systemically absorbed and cause bronchospasm in susceptible patients. The lesson: always ask about lung disease before prescribing any β-blocker.
mechanism.metaphor: β2 receptors are like the 'keep airways open' signal in the lungs. Let me show you: in an asthmatic, the airways are already twitchy and ready to clamp shut. That β2 signal is one of the few things keeping them open. giving a non-selective β-blocker is like cutting the rope holding up a drawbridge. The bridge (airways) crashes down, and nothing can get through. A cardioselective β-blocker is like fraying the rope but not cutting it completely — still risky, but less catastrophic. Isn't that a vivid image of why drug selectivity saves lives?

questions.q: A 70-year-old man with benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) has difficulty initiating urination and a weak urinary stream. He's prescribed an α1-blocker (tamsulosin). Which mechanism explains the therapeutic benefit?
options: Relaxation of internal urethral sphincter and prostate smooth muscle
Increased parasympathetic tone to the bladder
questions.explain: Come with me into the elegant pharmacology of urination. α1 receptors on the prostate and internal urethral sphincter mediate sympathetic contraction — keeping the sphincter closed and the prostate tissue tense. Block those receptors, and the smooth muscle relaxes — the outlet opens, urine flows. Let me show you why this works.

prereqs.q: The prostate gland surrounds the urethra and can obstruct urine flow when enlarged.
prereqs.explain: In BPH, the prostate enlarges and compresses the urethra like a hand squeezing a straw. both the bulk of the tissue AND the tension in the smooth muscle within the prostate contribute to obstruction. Reduce the tension, and flow improves even if the gland is still enlarged.

prereqs.q: α1 receptors mediate smooth muscle contraction in response to sympathetic activation.
prereqs.explain: α1 receptors are Gq-coupled — activation increases intracellular Ca²⁺ and causes smooth muscle contraction. in the prostate and bladder neck, this sympathetic tone keeps the outlet closed between urinations. Let yourself feel the sphincter grip.

optionExplains.text: The detrusor muscle (bladder wall) contracts to expel urine, yes — but that's mediated by PARASYMPATHETIC M3 receptors, not α1 receptors. α1-blockers don't increase detrusor contraction. Different mechanism.

Correct! Let me walk you through the autonomic control of urination and how α1-blockers help. NORMAL URINATION ANATOMY: Bladder has smooth muscle (detrusor) controlled by parasympathetic (M3 — contracts to void). Internal urethral sphincter (smooth muscle) and prostate smooth muscle both have α1 receptors (sympathetic — contract to maintain continence). External urethral sphincter (skeletal muscle) is under voluntary control. BPH PATHOLOGY: Enlarged prostate compresses urethra. Smooth muscle tone in prostate and sphincter adds DYNAMIC obstruction on top of the anatomical bulk. α1-BLOCKER MECHANISM: Tamsulosin (selective for α1A subtype in prostate) blocks α1 receptors → smooth muscle RELAXATION in prostate and bladder neck → decreased outlet resistance → improved urine flow. this doesn't shrink the prostate (that requires 5α-reductase inhibitors like finasteride), but it relieves the dynamic component of obstruction. Come closer: side effects include orthostatic hypotension (blocking vascular α1) and retrograde ejaculation (relaxed bladder neck allows semen to flow backward into bladder). Tamsulosin's α1A selectivity minimizes the BP effect. Isn't this targeted pharmacology beautiful?

Increasing parasympathetic tone would increase detrusor contraction (helpful for voiding), but that's not how α1-blockers work. α1-blockers act on the OUTLET, not the bladder muscle itself. Different site of action.

β-receptors in the bladder (β3) actually mediate RELAXATION of the detrusor during filling. Blocking them would impair storage, not improve emptying. Wrong receptor, wrong effect. α1-blockers target α1, not β.

mechanism.title: Autonomic Control of Micturition and α1-Blockers in BPH
mechanism.content: Come with me into the complex orchestration of urination. STORAGE PHASE (sympathetic dominance): Hypogastric nerve (T10-L2 sympathetic) — β3 receptors → detrusor relaxation (bladder fills without contracting). α1 receptors → internal sphincter contraction (outlet stays closed). Pudendal nerve (S2-S4 somatic) — external sphincter voluntarily contracted. VOIDING PHASE (parasympathetic dominance): Pelvic splanchnic nerves (S2-S4 parasympathetic) — M3 receptors → detrusor contraction. Sympathetic inhibited → sphincter relaxes. Voluntary relaxation of external sphincter. BPH OBSTRUCTION: STATIC component — enlarged gland tissue compresses urethra. DYNAMIC component — α1-mediated smooth muscle tone in prostate and bladder neck adds to resistance. α1-BLOCKER THERAPY: Blocks α1 receptors (especially α1A subtype predominant in prostate) → smooth muscle relaxation → decreased outlet resistance → improved peak flow rate, decreased residual urine, relief of symptoms (hesitancy, weak stream, frequency). DRUGS: Tamsulosin (α1A-selective — fewer cardiovascular effects), alfuzosin, doxazosin (non-selective — also lowers BP). SIDE EFFECTS: Orthostatic hypotension (vascular α1 blockade), dizziness, retrograde ejaculation (relaxed bladder neck), nasal congestion. Let yourself be drawn into the combination therapy: α1-blocker (immediate symptom relief) + 5α-reductase inhibitor like finasteride (shrinks prostate over months by blocking DHT). the MTOPS trial showed combination therapy superior to either alone for reducing BPH progression.
mechanism.metaphor: The prostate and bladder neck are like a drawstring bag that's too tight. Let me show you: the α1 receptors are constantly pulling the drawstring closed. An α1-blocker loosens the grip, letting the opening widen so urine can flow out more easily. it doesn't make the bag smaller (that's what finasteride does over time), but it makes the opening less constricted right away. Isn't that elegant symptomatic relief?

questions.q: A 28-year-old woman presents to the ER after eating wild mushrooms. She has profuse salivation, lacrimation, urination, diarrhea, sweating, and miosis (pinpoint pupils). Which receptor type has been overstimulated?
questions.explain: Come with me into this classic toxidrome. The constellation of SLUDGE (Salivation, Lacrimation, Urination, Defecation, GI distress) plus miosis and sweating screams MUSCARINIC overstimulation. She likely ate Amanita muscaria or Inocybe mushrooms containing muscarine. Let me show you why this activates the parasympathetic system.

prereqs.q: Muscarine is a toxin from certain mushrooms that mimics acetylcholine at muscarinic receptors.
prereqs.explain: Muscarine binds to and activates muscarinic cholinergic receptors, producing parasympathetic effects. this is where the receptor got its name. The mushroom toxin taught us about this receptor subtype. Let yourself see the pharmacological history written in poisonings.

prereqs.q: Parasympathetic activation increases secretions from glands and increases smooth muscle activity in the gut and bladder.
prereqs.explain: The parasympathetic 'rest and digest' response cranks up secretory activity (saliva, tears, digestive juices) and GI motility. when these systems are overstimulated, you get excessive secretions and diarrhea. Let yourself feel the parasympathetic overdrive.

optionExplains.text: Nicotinic receptors at ganglia, if overstimulated, would cause BOTH sympathetic AND parasympathetic activation (since both divisions use nicotinic receptors at ganglia) — you'd see mixed effects. This patient has pure parasympathetic effects. Wrong receptor location.

α1 receptor overstimulation would cause hypertension, vasoconstriction, pupil DILATION, and urinary retention — the OPPOSITE of what this patient has. This is sympathetic, not parasympathetic. Wrong division entirely.

Correct! Let me walk you through this poisoning. MUSCARINE TOXICITY activates M3 muscarinic receptors at parasympathetic target organs: EXOCRINE GLANDS (M3) → excessive secretion: Salivary glands → drooling. Lacrimal glands → tearing. Bronchial glands → bronchorrhea. Sweat glands → sweating (though sweat glands are sympathetic, they're cholinergic with muscarinic receptors — the exception). SMOOTH MUSCLE (M3) → contraction: GI tract → increased motility, cramping, diarrhea. Bladder detrusor → urination. Bronchi → bronchoconstriction (respiratory distress). EYES (M3) → pupillary sphincter contracts → miosis (pinpoint pupils), ciliary muscle contracts (blurred vision). CARDIOVASCULAR (M2) → bradycardia (though often obscured by compensatory tachycardia from hypotension). the classic SLUDGE mnemonic (Salivation, Lacrimation, Urination, Defecation, GI distress, Emesis) plus the Killer B's (Bradycardia, Bronchospasm, Bronchorrhea) describe cholinergic toxicity. Come closer: TREATMENT is atropine (muscarinic antagonist) — it competitively blocks the muscarine, reversing the parasympathetic overdrive. Dose is titrated to symptom resolution, especially respiratory secretions. Isn't this a beautiful example of receptor-based pharmacology saving lives?
β2 receptor overstimulation would cause bronchodilation, vasodilation, and tremor — sympathetic effects opposite to what this patient shows. Wrong receptors, wrong division.

mechanism.title: Cholinergic Toxicity: Muscarinic Overstimulation
mechanism.content: Let yourself be immersed in cholinergic toxidromes. CAUSES: Muscarinic agonist poisoning — muscarine-containing mushrooms (Amanita muscaria, Inocybe, Clitocybe), organophosphate/carbamate insecticides (inhibit acetylcholinesterase → ACh accumulation), nerve agents (sarin, VX), pilocarpine overdose. MUSCARINIC EFFECTS: M3 (Gq-coupled): Smooth muscle contraction — GI (cramping, diarrhea, involuntary defecation), bladder (urinary urgency/incontinence), bronchi (bronchoconstriction, wheezing), pupils (miosis — pinpoint pupils). Gland secretion — salivary, lacrimal, bronchial, sweat (even though sweat glands are sympathetic, they're cholinergic). M2 (Gi-coupled): Cardiac — bradycardia, AV block, hypotension. NICOTINIC EFFECTS (with organophosphates/nerve agents, which also affect nicotinic receptors): Muscle fasciculations, weakness, paralysis (NM receptors at neuromuscular junction), autonomic ganglia stimulation then blockade. CNS EFFECTS (with agents that cross blood-brain barrier): Confusion, seizures, respiratory depression. DIAGNOSIS: Clinical presentation (SLUDGE + Killer B's), low plasma cholinesterase (organophosphates), elevated RBC cholinesterase. TREATMENT: ATROPINE (muscarinic antagonist) — initial dose 2-5 mg IV, repeat every 5-10 minutes until secretions dry up. May need massive doses (hundreds of mg over hours). PRALIDOXIME (2-PAM) for organophosphates — reactivates cholinesterase if given early. Benzodiazepines for seizures. Supportive care — airway management, ventilation. Come with me into the contrast with NICOTINIC toxicity: purely nicotinic agonists (like nicotine overdose from insecticides) cause muscle fasciculations, weakness, sympathetic/parasympathetic ganglion effects. organophosphates produce BOTH muscarinic AND nicotinic effects, making them especially deadly.
mechanism.metaphor: Muscarinic poisoning is like someone turned on ALL the parasympathetic faucets at once and broke the handles so they won't turn off. Let me show you: tears flowing, saliva pouring, bowels emptying, bladder releasing — every secretion and every smooth muscle contracting uncontrollably. atropine is the emergency plumber who goes around shutting off each faucet by blocking the receptors. Isn't that a vivid image of pharmacological rescue?

questions.q: A patient develops severe bradycardia (heart rate 35 bpm) and hypotension after a surgical vagal nerve stimulator malfunction. The anesthesiologist administers atropine IV. Which mechanism explains the therapeutic effect?
options: Atropine stimulates β1 receptors to increase heart rate
Atropine blocks M2 muscarinic receptors, removing vagal inhibition of the SA node
Atropine activates nicotinic receptors at autonomic ganglia
questions.explain: Come with me into this elegant rescue. The vagus nerve was firing excessively, dumping ACh onto M2 receptors in the SA node and profoundly slowing the heart. Atropine is a competitive muscarinic antagonist — it blocks those M2 receptors, removing the parasympathetic brake. Let me show you why the heart rate immediately rises.

prereqs.q: The vagus nerve releases ACh onto M2 receptors in the heart to slow heart rate.
prereqs.explain: Parasympathetic control of the heart is via the vagus nerve. ACh binding to M2 receptors in the SA and AV nodes hyperpolarizes the cells and slows pacemaker firing. This is the 'rest and digest' brake on your heart. Remove it, and the heart speeds up.

prereqs.q: Atropine is a muscarinic receptor antagonist (blocker).
prereqs.explain: Atropine competitively blocks muscarinic cholinergic receptors throughout the body. it prevents ACh from binding, effectively shutting down parasympathetic effects at target organs. Let yourself see the molecular blockade.

optionExplains.text: Atropine doesn't stimulate β1 receptors — those are adrenergic (activated by NE/epinephrine), not cholinergic. Atropine works by BLOCKING parasympathetic effects, not by activating sympathetic receptors. Different mechanism entirely.

Correct! Let me walk you through vagal bradycardia and its reversal. EXCESSIVE VAGAL STIMULATION: Vagus nerve releases ACh → M2 muscarinic receptors on SA node and AV node → Gi-coupled cascade: Opens K⁺ channels (GIRK) → hyperpolarization → slower spontaneous depolarization. Decreases cAMP → reduced If and ICa,L → slower pacemaker activity. Result: profound bradycardia (heart rate <40 bpm), prolonged AV conduction (PR prolongation, even AV block), decreased cardiac output → hypotension, syncope. ATROPINE MECHANISM: Competitive antagonist at ALL muscarinic receptors (M1-M5). In the heart, blocks M2 → removes parasympathetic inhibition → SA node fires at intrinsic rate (~100 bpm) → heart rate increases. DOSE: 0.5-1 mg IV, can repeat every 3-5 minutes up to 3 mg total. Onset within 1-2 minutes. too low a dose (<0.5 mg) can paradoxically cause bradycardia (central effect or peripheral M1 presynaptic effects). Come closer: other muscarinic blockade effects occur — dry mouth (blocked salivary M3), blurred vision (blocked ciliary M3), urinary retention (blocked bladder M3), decreased GI motility. The classic atropine toxicity: 'hot as a hare, dry as a bone, red as a beet, blind as a bat, mad as a hatter.' In this emergency, the cardiac effect is life-saving despite the side effects. Isn't this receptor pharmacology at its most critical?

Atropine doesn't increase sympathetic tone — it blocks parasympathetic effects. The sympathetic system doesn't need activation here; it just needs the parasympathetic brake removed. The heart will then respond to whatever baseline sympathetic tone is present. Different mechanism.

Atropine blocks MUSCARINIC receptors, not nicotinic. Ganglionic nicotinic receptors aren't the issue here — the problem is at the target organ (heart) where M2 receptors are being overstimulated. Wrong receptor subtype.

mechanism.title: Vagal Bradycardia and Muscarinic Blockade
mechanism.content: Come with me into the autonomic control of heart rate and its pharmacological manipulation. NORMAL VAGAL TONE: At rest, the vagus nerve fires continuously, releasing ACh onto M2 receptors in the SA node. This parasympathetic 'brake' keeps resting heart rate ~70 bpm instead of the intrinsic SA node rate of ~100 bpm. The vagus dominates at rest. EXCESSIVE VAGAL ACTIVITY causes: Severe sinus bradycardia (<40 bpm), AV block (prolonged or blocked AV conduction), asystole (in extreme cases). CLINICAL TRIGGERS: Vasovagal syncope (sudden emotional/pain stimulus), carotid sinus hypersensitivity, increased intracranial pressure (Cushing reflex), certain medications (cholinesterase inhibitors, digoxin), vagal nerve stimulator malfunction. M2 RECEPTOR MECHANISM: Gi-coupled GPCR. Activation: Opens GIRK (inward rectifier K⁺ channels) → K⁺ efflux → hyperpolarization (membrane potential becomes more negative) → slower rate of spontaneous depolarization → decreased SA node firing rate. Inhibits adenylyl cyclase → decreased cAMP → reduced If (funny current) and ICa,L → slower pacemaker depolarization. Decreases AV nodal conduction velocity. ATROPINE REVERSAL: Competitive antagonist at M2 receptors → ACh cannot bind → vagal inhibition removed → heart rate increases to intrinsic rate or higher (if sympathetic tone is also present). SA node fires faster, AV conduction improves. DOSE and KINETICS: Adult dose 0.5-1 mg IV bolus, repeat PRN. Pediatric dose 0.02 mg/kg (minimum 0.1 mg). Onset <2 minutes, duration 2-4 hours. SIDE EFFECTS from non-selective muscarinic blockade: Tachycardia (desired in bradycardia, excessive at high doses), xerostomia (dry mouth), mydriasis and cycloplegia (dilated pupils, blurred near vision), urinary retention, constipation, hyperthermia (no sweating), CNS effects (confusion, agitation at high doses). Let yourself be drawn into the paradox: very LOW doses of atropine (<0.5 mg) can transiently worsen bradycardia via central or presynaptic mechanisms — always use adequate doses. if atropine fails to reverse bradycardia, use EPINEPHRINE (β1 stimulation) or TRANSCUTANEOUS PACING.
mechanism.metaphor: The vagus nerve is like a hand pressing the brake pedal on your heart. Let me show you: excessive vagal stimulation is like someone standing on the brake pedal with all their weight — the heart nearly stops. Atropine doesn't press the accelerator; it just removes the heavy foot from the brake. once the brake is released, the heart naturally speeds back up to its baseline pace. Isn't that a beautiful distinction between removing inhibition versus adding stimulation?
